topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the brutal finality of a dropped stone. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, and the headline screamed my name, but the byline belonged to a stranger. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the fluorescent lights of my small apartment seem too bright, too harsh. I reread the text, my fingers trembling slightly over the screen. Every carefully chosen sentence, every nuanced argument I had poured my soul into, was now attributed to someone else. A slow, heavy breath escaped my lungs, rattling in my chest. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the worn rug, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. I needed air, needed distance, but every direction felt equally suffocating.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels blurred. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. It wasn't just a mistake; it was a complete erasure of my contribution, a theft of my voice. My hands began to shake uncontrollably, the tremor escalating until I had to grip the edge of the desk to steady myself. I sank onto the sofa, pulling my knees to my chest, trying to make myself small. A low, ragged sound escaped my throat—a sound somewhere between a whimper and a choked sob. I kept glancing toward the door, half-expecting someone to walk in, to confront me, or perhaps just to confirm the impossible reality staring back at me in the glow of the monitor.

[story 3]

When I finally read the attribution, the world seemed to tilt on its axis. A strange, hollow sensation settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a long, slow exhale that didn't quite clear my throat. My vision narrowed, focusing intensely on the offending line, the borrowed words that now bore the weight of my intent. I started meticulously shuffling the papers on my desk, smoothing the edges of the printouts with obsessive, repetitive motions. Each movement was a frantic attempt to impose order on a chaos that had utterly dismantled my sense of self. I felt a deep, unsettling chill spreading from my core outward.
